tossups.question: Note to players: Composer and genre required. The third and fourth of these works heavily inspired a different composer’s piece Métamorphoses nocturnes to the extent that it was dubbed as the next of these works. One of these works uses the time signature “four plus two plus three” in its scherzo (“SKIRT-so”) and “three plus two plus two plus three” in its trio, which its composer borrowed from Bulgarian aksak. The first of these works opens with a Lento fugue that its composer referred to as (*) “funeral dirge” in a letter to Stefi Geyer. The last of these chamber works has all four movements beginning with a passage marked Mesto and does not follow the “arch form” used in its composer’s previous works in the genre. A movement in the fourth of these works is performed entirely in its composer’s namesake “snap pizzicato”. For 10 points, name these chamber works for four performers by the Hungarian composer of the Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.question_sanitized: Note to players: Composer and genre required. The third and fourth of these works heavily inspired a different composer's piece Metamorphoses nocturnes to the extent that it was dubbed as the next of these works. One of these works uses the time signature "four plus two plus three" in its scherzo ("SKIRT-so") and "three plus two plus two plus three" in its trio, which its composer borrowed from Bulgarian aksak. The first of these works opens with a Lento fugue that its composer referred to as (*) "funeral dirge" in a letter to Stefi Geyer. The last of these chamber works has all four movements beginning with a passage marked Mesto and does not follow the "arch form" used in its composer's previous works in the genre. A movement in the fourth of these works is performed entirely in its composer's namesake "snap pizzicato". For 10 points, name these chamber works for four performers by the Hungarian composer of the Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.answer: string quartets by Béla Bartók [accept Bartók Béla in place of “Béla Bartók”] (Métamorphoses nocturnes is Ligeti’s first string quartet.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: string quartets by Bela Bartok [accept Bartok Bela in place of "Bela Bartok"] (Metamorphoses nocturnes is Ligeti's first string quartet.)

tossups.question: In a piano piece by this composer, increasing rhythmic divisions build into a rapid quintuplet ostinato to depict a chase. That piece by this composer opens with ultra-low clusters imitating “Drums and Pipes.” The last seventeen measures of this composer’s third piano concerto were finished along with his viola concerto by his student Tibor Serly. This composer’s slow movements often employ atmospheric imitations of natural sound in a (*) “night music” style. An “interrupted intermezzo” appears in a piece by this composer whose second movement assigns different intervals to pairs of instruments. This “father of ethnomusicology” used an Edison machine to collect field recordings with his compatriot Zoltán Kodály. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of a Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a piano piece by this composer, increasing rhythmic divisions build into a rapid quintuplet ostinato to depict a chase. That piece by this composer opens with ultra-low clusters imitating "Drums and Pipes." The last seventeen measures of this composer's third piano concerto were finished along with his viola concerto by his student Tibor Serly. This composer's slow movements often employ atmospheric imitations of natural sound in a (*) "night music" style. An "interrupted intermezzo" appears in a piece by this composer whose second movement assigns different intervals to pairs of instruments. This "father of ethnomusicology" used an Edison machine to collect field recordings with his compatriot Zoltan Kodaly. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of a Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.answer: Béla Bartók [or Béla Viktor János Bartók] (The first line refers to Out of Doors.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Bela Bartok [or Bela Viktor Janos Bartok] (The first line refers to Out of Doors.)

tossups.question: According to a compatriot, this composer marked a “return to life” by composing a chamber piece whose “funeral dirge” first movement references his unrequited love for the violinist Stefi Geyer. This composer included a “recruiting dance” and a slow movement titled “relaxation” in his Contrasts for clarinet trio, written for Benny Goodman. Aksak time signatures like 4+3+2 over 8 feature in a string quartet by this composer that characteristically uses an arch form. This composer used the expression marking mesto in each movement of his sixth and final string quartet. This composer quoted Shostakovich’s Leningrad Symphony in the intermezzo interrotto fourth movement of a work that includes a “Game of Pairs.” For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.answer: Béla Bartók [or Béla Viktor János Bartók] (The quote in the first line is from Zoltán Kodály.)

packet.name: J – Berkeley B, Bristol A, Columbia B, Florida B, Imperial A, Mississippi State, Northwestern, Texas A&M A

tossups.answer_sanitized: Bela Bartok [or Bela Viktor Janos Bartok] (The quote in the first line is from Zoltan Kodaly.)
tossups.question_sanitized: According to a compatriot, this composer marked a "return to life" by composing a chamber piece whose "funeral dirge" first movement references his unrequited love for the violinist Stefi Geyer. Aksak time signatures like 4+3+2 over 8 feature in a string quartet by this composer that characteristically uses an arch form. This composer used the expression marking mesto in each movement of his sixth and final string quartet. This composer quoted Shostakovich's Leningrad Symphony in the intermezzo interrotto fourth movement of a work that includes a "Game of Pairs." For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of Concerto for Orchestra.

tossups.question: In a five-part piano collection by this composer, the Lento fourth piece is written with three staves as follows: the lower two staves are interspersed with appoggia·turated chromatic tone clusters, and the highest staff intermittently slips in and out of the C-sharp Dorian mode. The slow movement of this composer’s Piano Sonata features a handful of notes below A0 (“A-zero”), a standard piano’s lowest note. This composer’s Piano Concerto No. 1 begins with timpani beats over the soloist’s low (*) B-naturals, after which a menacing brass fanfare enters. This composer wrote all of those pieces in 1926, his “piano year.” Ferocious performances of this composer’s music have led Yefim Bronfman and Rui Urayama to bleed all over the keyboard. This composer wrote Allegro barbaro and Out of Doors. For 10 points, name this Hungarian ethnomusicologist who developed a style of spooky “night music.”
tossups.answer: Béla Bartók [or Bartók Béla; or Béla Viktor János Bartók or Bartók Béla Viktor János]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Bela Bartok [or Bartok Bela; or Bela Viktor Janos Bartok or Bartok Bela Viktor Janos]
tossups.question_sanitized: In a five-part piano collection by this composer, the Lento fourth piece is written with three staves as follows: the lower two staves are interspersed with appoggiaturated chromatic tone clusters, and the highest staff intermittently slips in and out of the C-sharp Dorian mode. The slow movement of this composer's Piano Sonata features a handful of notes below A0 ("A-zero"), a standard piano's lowest note. This composer wrote Allegro barbaro and Out of Doors. For 10 points, name this Hungarian ethnomusicologist who developed a style of spooky "night music."

tossups.question: One of these performers whistles the hymn “Will There Be Any Stars In My Crown?” in the Night-Spell I (“one”) movement of a 12-movement suite. One of these performers shouts, “Chri-ste!” (“KREE-stay”) in a Crucifixus movement literally shaped like a cross, part of a zodiac-inspired piece by George Crumb. Square note heads indicate that these performers should play harmonics in a set of 153 increasingly hard pieces by Béla Bartók. Before playing Sonatas and Interludes, these performers use screws, bolts, and rubber chunks to “prepare” their instrument. One of these performers, David Tudor, barely interacted with his instrument at the premiere of John Cage’s 4’33” (“four minutes thirty-three seconds”). For 10 points, name these performers who play a common keyboard instrument with 88 keys.
tossups.answer: pianists [or piano players; accept prepared piano players; prompt on musicians] (The first two lines refer to Crumb’s Makrokosmos. The third line refers to Bartók’s Mikrokosmos.)

packet.name: 06 - William and Mary A, Florida A, UIUC B, Virginia Tech A

tossups.answer_sanitized: pianists [or piano players; accept prepared piano players; prompt on musicians] (The first two lines refer to Crumb's Makrokosmos. The third line refers to Bartok's Mikrokosmos.)

tossups.question: Note to players: Composer and genre required. In the third of these pieces, the last cello chord is a quadruple stop in which each string plays a half-step higher than open. The second movement of the second of these works opens with diminished fifth alternations of F and B, followed by the ascending sixteenth notes [read slowly] “E, F, A, B-flat.” The last two of these works, which are not by a member of the Second Viennese School, were premiered by an ensemble led by Rudolf Kolisch. The scherzo of the fifth of these works is in (4+3+2)/8 (“four-plus-three-plus-two-eight”) time, and was influenced by Bulgarian folk music. Each movement of the last of these works has an introduction marked “Mesto.” The extensive use of “snap pizzicato” in the finale of the fourth of these led that technique to be renamed for their composer. For 10 points, name these chamber works, the sixth and last of which was completed two years before their composer’s 1943 Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.answer: string quartets of Béla (Viktor János) Bartók [both parts required; prompt on partial answer]
tossups.answer_sanitized: string quartets of Bela (Viktor Janos) Bartok [both parts required; prompt on partial answer]

tossups.question: Recordings made by this composer are re-interpreted on a 2020 album by Lucian Ban, John Surman, and Mat Maneri. An oft-orchestrated six-movement piano suite by this composer begins with two movements depicting "stick" and "sash" dances. This composer's recordings of "rain-begging" songs in Turkey are among those he later brought to Columbia University as a professor. The first movement of this composer's clarinet-piano-violin trio Contrasts is a (*) verbunkos ("VAIR-boon-kosh"), a dance which he determined was Romani in origin. This composer of Romanian Folk Dances inserted his characteristic "night music" into several of his peasant dances, and analyzed his country's folk songs with his colleague Zoltan Kodály ("ZOL-tahn KOH-dye"). For 10 points, name this composer who added Hungarian tunes to his Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.question_sanitized: Recordings made by this composer are re-interpreted on a 2020 album by Lucian Ban, John Surman, and Mat Maneri. For 10 points, name this composer who added Hungarian tunes to his Concerto for Orchestra.

tossups.question: Yvonne Loriod (“lore-YO”) only took eight days to learn a concerto by this composer that András Schiff described as “finger-breaking” and “the single most difficult piece” he’s played. This composer died before orchestrating the end of his Third Piano Concerto, which uses a technique he termed “polymodal chromaticism.” This composer also left sketches of a concerto for William Primrose that was completed by his student Tibor Serly. The second movement of a piece by this composer opens with a drum played without snares, followed by the bassoons playing a jaunty melody a minor sixth apart. This composer’s second piano concerto exemplifies his “arch form,” and its slow movement is in his “night music” style. The second movement of a piece by this composer is a “Game of Pairs.” For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of a Concerto for Orchestra and the opera Duke Bluebeard’s Castle.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Bela Bartok [or Bela Viktor Janos Bartok]
tossups.question_sanitized: Yvonne Loriod ("lore-YO") only took eight days to learn a concerto by this composer that Andras Schiff described as "finger-breaking" and "the single most difficult piece" he's played. This composer also left sketches of a concerto for William Primrose that was completed by his student Tibor Serly. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of a Concerto for Orchestra and the opera Duke Bluebeard's Castle.

tossups.question: The left hand repeats the chord “D G-sharp A” in quick eighth notes to open this composer’s piano piece “Ostinato.” This composer wrote a very short Opus 14 piano suite, and in another suite from his “piano year” of 1926, a run of chromatic grace notes up to the repeated semitone “D E-flat” represents the unka frog. “Parallel Motion” and various “Unison Melodies” appear in the first book of a collection of piano pieces by this composer, which he dedicated to his son Peter. The fourth movement of this composer’s Out of Doors exemplifies his “night music” style. He wrote “Six Dances in Bulgarian Rhythm” to cap off 153 pieces of increasing difficulty called Mikrokosmos. For 10 points, name this composer whose Allegro barbaro uses scales from his native Hungary.
tossups.question_sanitized: The left hand repeats the chord "D G-sharp A" in quick eighth notes to open this composer's piano piece "Ostinato." "Parallel Motion" and various "Unison Melodies" appear in the first book of a collection of piano pieces by this composer, which he dedicated to his son Peter. The fourth movement of this composer's Out of Doors exemplifies his "night music" style. He wrote "Six Dances in Bulgarian Rhythm" to cap off 153 pieces of increasing difficulty called Mikrokosmos. For 10 points, name this composer whose Allegro barbaro uses scales from his native Hungary.

tossups.question: The fast part of a 9-8 time first movement by this composer begins with the timpani playing alternating Cs and F-sharps, followed by three syncopated, quarter-note C major chords. This composer’s second piano concerto opens with the soloist playing a quick scale up to a trill that’s sustained under a trumpet fanfare. His first piano concerto begins with low Bs pounded out by the timpani and then by the soloist, horns, and trombones, at the bottom of their ranges. In a book on this composer’s music by Elliott Antokoletz, the opening of his third piano (*) concerto is analyzed as a mixture of E-Mixolydian and E-Dorian modes. He included an “Andante religioso” in his third piano concerto, which he wrote alongside his unfinished viola concerto while dying of leukemia. This composer’s notoriously difficult second piano concerto is built in an “arch form,” and his Sonata for Two Pianos and Percussion uses his “night music” style. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of a Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.question_sanitized: The fast part of a 9-8 time first movement by this composer begins with the timpani playing alternating Cs and F-sharps, followed by three syncopated, quarter-note C major chords. This composer's notoriously difficult second piano concerto is built in an "arch form," and his Sonata for Two Pianos and Percussion uses his "night music" style. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of a Concerto for Orchestra.

tossups.question: Composer and type of piece required. Elizabeth Sprague Coolidge commissioned the fifth of these pieces, whose Allegro vivace finale is interrupted by two instruments laconically playing the same melody a semitone apart, in A and B-flat. That piece's unpaired central movement is a scherzo featuring a 4-plus-2-plus-3 rhythm marked "alla bulgarese." All four movements of the last of these pieces have a slow Mesto introduction. The fourth of these pieces uses the "arch form" favored by its composer, and its fourth movement makes heavy use of its composer's namesake snap pizzicato. That piece was first performed in 1929 in Budapest. For 10 points, identify these six chamber pieces by the Hungarian composer of Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta and Concerto for Orchestra.
tossups.answer: string quartets by Béla Bartók [prompt on partial answers]
tossups.answer_sanitized: string quartets by Bela Bartok [prompt on partial answers]

tossups.question: In his sonata for this instrument, Dmitri Shostakovich subtitled the final movement adagio “in the memory of Beethoven” and quoted the Moonlight Sonata; that sonata for this instrument was Shostakovich’s final composition. A concerto for this instrument has a version completed by the composer’s son Peter, though the standard version was edited by Tibor Serly. A William Walton concerto for this instrument, dedicated “to Christabel,” was commissioned by Lionel Tertis. A piece for this instrument and orchestra contains a movement called “Orgy of the Brigands” and was written at the request of Niccolò Paganini. The soloist in Harold in Italy plays, for 10 points, what alto-clef instrument that forms a string quartet with two violins and a cello?
tossups.answer: viola [accept (Shostakovich’s) Viola Sonata or (Shostakovich’s) Sonata for Viola and Piano or (Bartók’s) Viola Concerto or (Walton’s) Viola Concerto]

packet.name: 13 - Columbia A + Carleton A + Michigan State

tossups.answer_sanitized: viola [accept (Shostakovich's) Viola Sonata or (Shostakovich's) Sonata for Viola and Piano or (Bartok's) Viola Concerto or (Walton's) Viola Concerto]

tossups.question: In an opera by this composer, the minor second interval is known as the “blood motif”. There is confusion as to whether the penultimate movement of one of his works is parodying Franz Lehar’s The Merry Widow or Shostakovich’s invasion theme from the Leningrad Symphony. This composer’s dissonant “night music” is found in the slow movements of many of his compositions. 153 pieces for piano of increasing difficulty are set in his Mikrokosmos and he also wrote Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta. For 10 points name this man who wrote Concerto for Orchestra, the Miraculous Mandarin and Bluebeard’s Castle, a composer from Hungary.
tossups.question_sanitized: In an opera by this composer, the minor second interval is known as the "blood motif". There is confusion as to whether the penultimate movement of one of his works is parodying Franz Lehar's The Merry Widow or Shostakovich's invasion theme from the Leningrad Symphony. This composer's dissonant "night music" is found in the slow movements of many of his compositions. 153 pieces for piano of increasing difficulty are set in his Mikrokosmos and he also wrote Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta. For 10 points name this man who wrote Concerto for Orchestra, the Miraculous Mandarin and Bluebeard's Castle, a composer from Hungary.

tossups.question: George Perle explored the use of tetrachordal set classes to modulate in these works by this composer in a paper about their symmetric formations. A movement in the first of these works begins with an accelerando as the viola and cello play in minor sixths, and then the violins play in major thirds. Two violins play G-string glissandi a quarter-tone apart in the burletta section of one of these works. The third is divided into "Prima Parte," "Seconda Parte," recapitulation of "Prima Parte," and a coda. The "alla bulgarese" scherzo of the fifth of is in 1.+2+3)/8 time. Each movement of the last of these works begins with a mesto theme, and some of these works are in the composer's characteristic arch form. The Allegretto pizzicato movement of the fourth of them uses the composer's namesake snap pizzicato. For 10 points, identify these six pieces for two violins, viola, and cello by a Hungarian composer.

Each movement of the last of these works begins with a mesto theme, and some of these works are in the composer's characteristic arch form. The Allegretto pizzicato movement of the fourth of them uses the composer's namesake snap pizzicato. For 10 points, identify these six pieces for two violins, viola, and cello by a Hungarian composer.

tossups.question: The horns play octaves to break from a piano and timpani duet at the beginning of this composer’s first concerto. G-sharp and C-sharp are added to a pantomime chord that is modified throughout “The Chase,” the final piece in a suite by this composer. A xylophone solo in one of his compositions exemplifies his style of “night music.” This composer of Music for Strings, Percussion, and (*) Celesta used the “intermezzo interrotto” fourth movement of one piece to parody the “invasion theme” from the Leningrad Symphony; that work is better known for featuring sets of two instruments playing together in its second movement “Game of Pairs.” This composer of Concerto for Orchestra arranged 153 piano pieces in order of difficulty for another collection. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of Duke Bluebeard’s Castle and Mikrokosmos.
tossups.question_sanitized: The horns play octaves to break from a piano and timpani duet at the beginning of this composer's first concerto. G-sharp and C-sharp are added to a pantomime chord that is modified throughout "The Chase," the final piece in a suite by this composer. A xylophone solo in one of his compositions exemplifies his style of "night music." This composer of Music for Strings, Percussion, and (*) Celesta used the "intermezzo interrotto" fourth movement of one piece to parody the "invasion theme" from the Leningrad Symphony; that work is better known for featuring sets of two instruments playing together in its second movement "Game of Pairs." This composer of Concerto for Orchestra arranged 153 piano pieces in order of difficulty for another collection. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of Duke Bluebeard's Castle and Mikrokosmos.

tossups.question: A character in one of this composer’s ballets is represented by trombone glissandi playing the interval of a descending minor third. The prologue of an opera by this composer opens with a motif drawn from the pentatonic collection “F#-A-B-C#-E” that’s referred to as that opera’s “Darkness” motif. Elliot Antokoletz argued that the leitmotif for that opera’s protagonist is a modal form of the theme this composer used to represent Stef Geyer in his frst Violin Concerto. In one ballet by this com-poser, tramps force a girl to seduce the wealthy title character, who refuses to die afer being repeatedly stabbed and hanged from a lamp hook. In his only opera the soprano encounters a lake of tears, a bloody torture chamber, and the title character’s three other wives behind the seventh door of the title location. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of The Mirac-ulous Mandarin and Duke Bluebeard’s Castle.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Bela Bartok [accept names in either order]

For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of The Mirac-ulous Mandarin and Duke Bluebeard's Castle.

tossups.question: The first part of this composer’s Two Portraits is the first movement of his first string quartet, which is dominated by the “love motif” inspired by the violinist Stefi Geyer. One work by him has a second movement in which pairs of instruments play the same tune in parallel intervals, while another features two string groups on both sides of the stage and has a third movement that uses timpani glissandi and the xylophone to invoke this composer’s (*) “night music” style. Yet another work by him begins with the “Six Unison Melodies” and is a collection of 153 piano pieces arranged by difficulty. In addition to Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta and Mikrokosmos, this man is also composed an opera in which a woman discovers her husband’s dead wives in the title location. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of Duke Bluebeard’s Castle.
tossups.question_sanitized: The first part of this composer's Two Portraits is the first movement of his first string quartet, which is dominated by the "love motif" inspired by the violinist Stefi Geyer. One work by him has a second movement in which pairs of instruments play the same tune in parallel intervals, while another features two string groups on both sides of the stage and has a third movement that uses timpani glissandi and the xylophone to invoke this composer's (*) "night music" style. Yet another work by him begins with the "Six Unison Melodies" and is a collection of 153 piano pieces arranged by difficulty. In addition to Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta and Mikrokosmos, this man is also composed an opera in which a woman discovers her husband's dead wives in the title location. For 10 points, name this Hungarian composer of Duke Bluebeard's Castle.

bonuses.leadin: This piece’s “Intermezzo interrotto” fourth movement features a trombone glissando that is not possible on modern instruments since it was originally written for a bass trombone pitched in F. For 10 points each:

parts: Name this work, whose fourth movement also includes a quote from Franz Lehár's The Merry Widow as a parody of Shostakovich’s earlier quote of the work in his Leningrad Symphony.

This composer drew inspiration from his country’s folk music for his Concerto for Orchestra. This Hungarian composer and ethnomusicologist composed The Miraculous Mandarin and is known for his style of “night music.”
In works like Concerto for Orchestra and The Miraculous Mandarin, Bartok frequently called for this technique in his brass parts, denoted by the instruction con sordino.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about specially-designed orchestral instruments, for 10 points each:
parts: Bruckner’s late symphonies use a “tuba” that this composer designed to bridge an acoustic gap between the French horns and trombones. Bruckner first heard this composer’s tuba in its debut at the first Bayreuth festival.

Charles Gounod’s St. Cecilia Mass is the only piece that calls for this instrument invented by Jean-Baptiste Vuillaume (“v’wee-YOHM”). The Montreal Symphony is the only ensemble that owns one of these instruments, which are lever-operated due to their size.

Many performances of this piece use a custom bass trombone with a second slide to play a B–F glissando in its fourth movement. A third bassoon adds a running countermelody to the recapitulation of an earlier movement in this piece that is based on Croatian part-singing.
answers: Richard Wagner [or Wilhelm Richard Wagner] (Wagner commissioned the Wagner tuba for his Ring Cycle.)
Béla Bartók’s Concerto for Orchestra (Bartók wrote that bass trombone part for an F bass trombone. Modern-day bass trombonists play B-flat bass trombones, on which that glissando is unplayable.)
answers_sanitized: Richard Wagner [or Wilhelm Richard Wagner] (Wagner commissioned the Wagner tuba for his Ring Cycle.)
octobass [or octobasse]
Bela Bartok's Concerto for Orchestra (Bartok wrote that bass trombone part for an F bass trombone. Modern-day bass trombonists play B-flat bass trombones, on which that glissando is unplayable.)

Many performances of this piece use a custom bass trombone with a second slide to play a B-F glissando in its fourth movement. A third bassoon adds a running countermelody to the recapitulation of an earlier movement in this piece that is based on Croatian part-singing.

bonuses.leadin: This city’s namesake orchestra – one of the American “Big Five” – cultivated a lush, velvety string sound under the Hungarian-born conductor Eugene Ormandy. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this city, where Ormandy directed that orchestra from 1938 to 1980 after taking over from Leopold Stokowski.
Ormandy led the Philadelphia Orchestra in the posthumous premiere of the third piano concerto by this fellow Hungarian. This composer also wrote The Miraculous Mandarin and a Concerto for Orchestra.

Prior to his appointment in Philadelphia, Ormandy directed the main orchestra in this other American city. In 2022, Osmo Vänskä (VEN-skeh) stepped down as music director of that same orchestra in this city after nearly 20 years at the helm, which were interrupted by a lockout from 2012 to 2014.
answers: Minneapolis [reject “St. Paul”] (In Ormandy’s time the orchestra was called the Minneapolis Symphony; it is now the Minnesota Orchestra.)
answers_sanitized: Philadelphia
Bela Bartok [accept names in either order]
Minneapolis [reject "St. Paul"] (In Ormandy's time the orchestra was called the Minneapolis Symphony; it is now the Minnesota Orchestra.)

bonuses.leadin: In a 1971 essay on “Tendencies in Modern Music” presented in Moscow, a composer defined this term using the juxtaposing and intersecting of contrasting musical idioms within Henri Pousseur’s Votre Faust. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this term referring to the internal anachronism within the works of a modern composer of many concerti grossi. A Gidon Kremer concert tour publicized this paradigm via its usage in its creator’s many controversial cadenzas to Beethoven’s Violin Concerto.

Schnittke’s first cadenza to the Beethoven Concerto includes quotations from this man’s two violin concerti. A different concerto by him quotes Shostakovich’s “invasion theme” in its fourth movement “intermezzo interrotto.”
Schnittke’s first cadenza also quotes the twelve-note tone row used in Alban Berg’s own violin concerto. Berg was a student of this Austrian serialist, the founder of the Second Viennese School.
answers: Béla Bartók (That is his Concerto for Orchestra.)
answers_sanitized: polystylism [or word forms, like polystyle or polystylistics]
Bela Bartok (That is his Concerto for Orchestra.)

bonuses.leadin: A short piece titled for this composer’s distinctive style of slow music, the fourth movement from his piano suite Out of Doors, is an early example of it because of its use of soft, mysterious dissonances. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this composer who used the term “night music” to describe the musical textures of many of his slow movements, which attempt to evoke quiet sounds from nature.

This composer’s seventh symphony, titled “Song of the Night,” features two middle movements also titled “Night Music.” This Austrian composer’s other symphonies include ones nicknamed “Titan” and “Resurrection.”

This composer’s 1954 Little Night Music is one of his most popular orchestral works along with his 1932 Partita, which ends with the lullaby “Naenia B.M.V.” This Italian serialist turned his choral work Songs of Imprisonment into the 1948 opera The Prisoner.
answers_sanitized: Bela Bartok
Gustav Mahler
Luigi Dallapiccola

bonuses.leadin: The “Stick Dance” recorded by two gypsy fiddlers and the popular “Brâul” (“brih-ool”) or “Sash Dance” are the first two of the authentic tunes accompanied by rich harmonies in this suite. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this piano suite of 6 short movements in a variety of modes based directly on 7 melodies collected near the Hungarian border with Transylvania. It was composed by a pioneering ethnomusicologist during World War I.

Like Bartók’s Rhapsody No. 1, the final dance, “Mărunțel” (“muh-roont-SELL”), mixes this “sharpest” mode with another mode of a similar name. This mode is built by raising the fourth degree of a major scale or taking 7 white keys starting from F.

The “acoustic scale,” named for its similarity to the overtone series, mixes Lydian and Mixolydian qualities, and is the fourth mode of this scale. When this type of minor scale ascends from G, the set of accidentals is B-flat, E-natural, and F-sharp.
answers: Romanian Folk Dances [or Six Romanian Folk Dances; or Rumanian Folk Dances; or Román népi táncok; or Rumänische Volkstänze; prompt on partial answer] (by Béla Bartók)

parts_sanitized: Name this composer, who also wrote the increasingly difficult Mikrokosmos.
This composer's first string quartet was heavily inspired by the Bartok quartets, but his later works were focused more on his theory of micropolyphony, such as in his Lontano.
Both Bartok and Ligeti are from this country. A series of "Rhapsodies" based on folk tunes in this country was written by its country's most famous composer, Franz Liszt.

bonuses.leadin: A female composer from this country wrote Metacosmos, a microtonal symphonic poem meant to evoke a black hole. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country also home to a film composer who scored Mandy, Arrival, and The Theory of Everything before his 2018 death.
The title of Icelandic composer Anna Thorvaldsdóttir’s Metacosmos might allude to Mikrokosmos, a series of piano pieces by this Hungarian composer who also wrote a Concerto for Orchestra.

Bartók’s Mikrokosmos and the constellations of the zodiac inspired Makrokosmos, a collection of piano pieces by this American composer who interpolated Schubert’s Death and the Maiden into his Black Angels string quartet.
answers_sanitized: Iceland [or Island] (The film composer is Johann Johannsson.)
Bela Bartok [or Bela Viktor JanosBartok]
George Crumb [or George Henry Jr. Crumb]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A female composer from this country wrote Metacosmos, a microtonal symphonic poem meant to evoke a black hole. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this country also home to a film composer who scored Mandy, Arrival, and The Theory of Everything before his 2018 death.
The title of Icelandic composer Anna Thorvaldsdottir's Metacosmos might allude to Mikrokosmos, a series of piano pieces by this Hungarian composer who also wrote a Concerto for Orchestra.

Bartok's Mikrokosmos and the constellations of the zodiac inspired Makrokosmos, a collection of piano pieces by this American composer who interpolated Schubert's Death and the Maiden into his Black Angels string quartet.

bonuses.leadin: Of the one-thousand-plus instruments that Antonio Stradivari made, it is estimated that over 900 of them were these instruments. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this highest-pitched and most common string instrument in a standard orchestra.
Violin players often utilize this technique of plucking the violin’s strings. Béla Bartók pioneered and names a version of this technique that is so percussive, it produces a “snap” sound.
The player must naturally allow the bow to bounce from the violin’s strings in this type of stroke that is often confused with the spiccato stroke.

packet.name: N - Chicago B, Georgia B, Oxford A, Stanford A, and W&M

answers_sanitized: violins
pizzicato [accept Bartok pizzicato or snap pizzicato; prompt on pizz]
sautille ("so-tee-YAY") stroke

bonuses.leadin: This piece opens with the piano introducing and the soloist arpeggiating a gentle, rocking E dominant ninth chord, a cyclic motif that returns in its 3rd and 4th movements, a recitative-fantasia and a canon. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this chamber piece in A major composed as wedding present for Eugène Ysaÿe (“oo-ZHEN ee-ZYE”), who premiered it from memory in a dark art museum in Brussels in 1886. A cello version of it is by Jules Delsart.

A 1910 Budapest visit influenced this composer’s last piece, a short 1917 violin sonata in a projected set of six sonatas he signed “musicien français” (“myoo-zeese-YANN fron-SAY”), including one for flute, viola, and harp. A cyclic motif unifies his String Quartet in G minor influenced by Grieg and premiered by the Ysaÿe Quartet.

This composer wrote a solo violin sonata with optional quarter tones for Yehudi Menuhin (“yeh-HOO-dee MEN-yoo-in”) a year after Concerto for Orchestra and a year before his death. This Hungarian ethnomusicologist wrote two violin sonatas and six influential, modern string quartets.
answers: Violin Sonata in A major by César Franck (“say-zar fronk”) [or Franck’s Sonata for Violin and Piano; prompt on partial answer]
answers_sanitized: Violin Sonata in A major by Cesar Franck ("say-zar fronk") [or Franck's Sonata for Violin and Piano; prompt on partial answer]
Claude Debussy
Bela Bartok
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This piece opens with the piano introducing and the soloist arpeggiating a gentle, rocking E dominant ninth chord, a cyclic motif that returns in its 3rd and 4th movements, a recitative-fantasia and a canon. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this chamber piece in A major composed as wedding present for Eugene Ysaye ("oo-ZHEN ee-ZYE"), who premiered it from memory in a dark art museum in Brussels in 1886. A cello version of it is by Jules Delsart.

A 1910 Budapest visit influenced this composer's last piece, a short 1917 violin sonata in a projected set of six sonatas he signed "musicien francais" ("myoo-zeese-YANN fron-SAY"), including one for flute, viola, and harp. A cyclic motif unifies his String Quartet in G minor influenced by Grieg and premiered by the Ysaye Quartet.

This composer wrote a solo violin sonata with optional quarter tones for Yehudi Menuhin ("yeh-HOO-dee MEN-yoo-in") a year after Concerto for Orchestra and a year before his death. This Hungarian ethnomusicologist wrote two violin sonatas and six influential, modern string quartets.

bonuses.leadin: In the death scene from this stage work, an offstage women’s chorus sings a wordless minor third. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this one-act pantomime ballet in which thieves force a girl to attract passing men by dancing ‘lockspiels’ or ‘decoy dances.’ The title character proves impossible for the thieves to kill until the girl accepts his love.
This 20th-century Hungarian composer wrote The Miraculous Mandarin and a Concerto for Orchestra. In his fourth string quartet, he instructed players to snap the strings against the fingerboard.

Bartok's Concerto for Orchestra was premiered by this city's Symphony Orchestra, whose director Sergei Koussevitzky had commissioned the piece. That orchestra hosts a summer music festival at Tanglewood, and this city's Pops Orchestra was conducted by Arthur Fiedler for many years.
answers_sanitized: The Miraculous Mandarin [or A csodalotos mandarin; or Der wunderbare Mandarin]
Bela Bartok [or Bela Viktor Janos Bartok]
Boston [accept Boston Symphony Orchestra or Boston Pops Orchestra]

This 20th-century Hungarian composer wrote The Miraculous Mandarin and a Concerto for Orchestra. In his fourth string quartet, he instructed players to snap the strings against the fingerboard.

Bartok's Concerto for Orchestra was premiered by this city's Symphony Orchestra, whose director Sergei Koussevitzky had commissioned the piece. That orchestra hosts a summer music festival at Tanglewood, and this city's Pops Orchestra was conducted by Arthur Fiedler for many years.

bonuses.leadin: Olivier Messiaen dubbed this collection “Mode 2.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this pitch collection that can be constructed as two adjacent diminished seventh chords. It includes four each of major and minor triads at even intervals of a minor third.
This composer frequently used octatonic and hexatonic collections. He was also one of the first people to systematically use audio recording to capture folksong.
One of Bartók’s most thoroughly octatonic pieces is “Harvest Song,” a piece for two of these high string instruments. From lowest to highest, this instrument is tuned “G-D-A-E.”
answers_sanitized: octatonic scale
Bela (Viktor Janos) Bartok
violins [or fiddles]

bonuses.leadin: Swiss musicologist Marcel Cellier (“sell-YAY”) brought attention to the “open-throated” singing styles of folk music from this country, which often has asymmetrical time signatures. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country whose State Television Female Vocal Choir worked with Cellier on that project. Six dances written in rhythms from this country appear at the end of Volume VI (“six”) of Mikrokosmos.
This Hungarian composer and pioneering musicologist created Mikrokosmos, Romanian Folk Dances, and Concerto for Orchestra, which uses his characteristic eerie “night music” style.

This Hungarian composed a Concerto for Orchestra partially inspired by the Hungarian folk material that he collected in six volumes. In 1945, Hungary’s government implemented the pedagogical method named for this composer of Háry János (“HA-ree YAH-nohsh”).
answers_sanitized: Bulgaria [or Republic of Bulgaria; or Kingdom of Bulgaria]
Bela Bartok [accept names in either order]
Zoltan Kodaly ("KOH-dye") [accept names in either order]

This Hungarian composer and pioneering musicologist created Mikrokosmos, Romanian Folk Dances, and Concerto for Orchestra, which uses his characteristic eerie "night music" style.

This Hungarian composed a Concerto for Orchestra partially inspired by the Hungarian folk material that he collected in six volumes. In 1945, Hungary's government implemented the pedagogical method named for this composer of Hary Janos ("HA-ree YAH-nohsh").

bonuses.leadin: Nikos Kazantzakis’s character Zorba plays a santuri, which is a Greek word for this instrument. For 10 points each:
parts: Give the common English name for this instrument from the dulcimer family, used in many folk music genres from Central Europe. It is played using two hammers.
Cimbaloms are common in klezmer bands, which usually sing songs in this German-derived language spoken by many Ashkenazi Jews.

This composer included a cimbalom in an arrangement of his Rhapsody No. 1 for violin and orchestra. As a pioneering ethnomusicologist, this frequent user of “night music” collected much music from Eastern Europe, such as that used in his six Romanian Folk Dances.
answers_sanitized: cimbalom [prompt on hammered dulcimer]
Yiddish
Bela Bartok

bonuses.leadin: In Peter Maxwell Davies’s Eight Songs for a Mad King, this type of cast is accompanied by an onstage Pierrot ensemble. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this unusual casting decision employed in the operas Erwartung and La voix humaine.
La voix humaine is an opera by this composer. Another of his operas ends with the execution of the title convent of nuns during the French Revolution.
This Hungarian composer’s opera Duke Bluebeard’s Castle features a much larger cast of two roles: the title character and his new wife Judith.
answers: there is only one role [or it is a monodrama; or obvious equivalents]

bonuses.leadin: This composer's Violin Concerto No. 2 begins with the harp playing a soft chord eight times, and includes a second movement in theme-and-variations form. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this composer whose Piano Concerto No. 3 includes a chorale-like "Adagio religioso" as its central movement. The third movement of his Piano Concerto No. 2 begins with a loud bass drum THWACK.
Bartok's String Quartet No. 4 calls for his namesake "snap" variety of this technique, in which the strings are plucked rather than bowed.
Bassoons open the "Game of Pairs" movement of this Bartok piece, whose paradoxical title originates from his desire to showcase each instrument of the title ensemble.
answers_sanitized: Bela Bartok [or Bela Viktor Janos Bartok]
pizzicato [or snap pizzicato; or Bartok pizzicato]
Concerto for Orchestra
